examples.explanation: Start: i=0, j=0. p[0]='a', s[0]='a' match → i=1, j=1. j=1 (pattern end), i=1 (string not end) → return false.

Start: i=0, j=0. p[0]='*' → branch 1: skip '*' (j=1) → j=1 (pattern end), i=0 (string not end) → false; branch 2: use '*' match s[0] (i=1, j=0) → p[0]='*' again → branch 2.1: skip (j=1) → j=1 end, i=1 not end → false; branch 2.2: use match s[1] (i=2, j=0) → p[0]='*' → branch 2.2.1: skip (j=1) → j=1 end, i=2 end → true. Return true.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(m+n))
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible matches by exploring both matching and skipping options for '*' characters, leading to exponential time. Use it only for understanding the problem, as it's too slow for practical cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Wildcard Matching
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible matches with exponential complexity.
ladders.explanation: The brute force recursion approach solves wildcard matching by exploring all possible ways to match the pattern against the string. The intuition is that for each character position, we have decisions to make based on the pattern character: if it's a regular character, we must match it exactly; if it's '?', we can match any single character; if it's '*', we have two options—either skip the '*' (match it to zero characters) or use it to match one character and keep it active for more. This creates a binary decision tree at each '*', leading to exponential possibilities. The algorithm uses recursion with two pointers: i for string index and j for pattern index. Base cases: if both pointers reach the end, it's a match; if pattern ends but string doesn't, it's not a match (unless remaining pattern is all '*', handled in recursion). Recursive cases: if pattern[j] is '*', we try both skipping it (j+1) and using it to match current character (i+1, j same). If pattern[j] is '?' or matches string[i], we move both pointers forward. Time complexity is O(2^(m+n)) in worst case (many '*' characters) because each '*' creates two recursive branches. Space complexity is O(m+n) for recursion stack depth. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem but impractical for large inputs due to exponential growth. Connection to L1: This full solution implements the recursive matching subroutine from L1 within the complete problem context. Common mistakes include not handling multiple '*' consecutively correctly (they should be treated as single '*'), and missing base cases for empty strings/patterns. Real code pattern: def isMatch(s, i, p, j): if j == len(p): return i == len(s); if p[j] == '*': return isMatch(s, i, p, j+1) or (i < len(s) and isMatch(s, i+1, p, j)); if i < len(s) and (p[j] == '?' or p[j] == s[i]): return isMatch(s, i+1, p, j+1); return False.

examples.trace: Start: i=0, j=0. p[0]='a', s[0]='a' match → i=1, j=1. j=1 (pattern end), i=1 (string not end) → return false.

Start: i=0, j=0. p[0]='*' → branch 1: skip '*' (j=1) → j=1 (pattern end), i=0 (string not end) → false; branch 2: use '*' match s[0] (i=1, j=0) → p[0]='*' again → branch 2.1: skip (j=1) → j=1 end, i=1 not end → false; branch 2.2: use match s[1] (i=2, j=0) → p[0]='*' → branch 2.2.1: skip (j=1) → j=1 end, i=2 end → true. Return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Matching Function
ladders.desc: Core recursive function that decides match at current positions.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function that takes current indices i (string) and j (pattern) and returns whether the substring from i matches the subpattern from j. The intuition is breaking the problem into smaller subproblems: at each step, we only need to decide how to handle the current pattern character relative to the current string character. For '*', we explore both possibilities (skip or use), creating the exponential tree. The algorithm: Base case: if j reaches pattern end, return true only if i also at string end. If pattern[j] is '*', return OR of two recursive calls: skip '*' (call with j+1) or match current char (if i not at end, call with i+1, j same). If pattern[j] is '?' or equals string[i] (and i not at end), return call with i+1, j+1. Otherwise return false. Time complexity for this function in isolation is O(2^(m+n)) due to recursive branches, but analyzing it as a subproblem, its cost per call is O(1) with exponential calls. Space is O(m+n) for stack. Connection to L2: This subroutine implements the core logic of decision-making at each position. Common mistakes: forgetting to check i < len(s) before accessing s[i], or not handling empty string with '*' pattern correctly. Real code pattern: def match(s, i, p, j): if j == len(p): return i == len(s); if p[j] == '*': return match(s, i, p, j+1) or (i < len(s) and match(s, i+1, p, j)); if i < len(s) and (p[j] == '?' or p[j] == s[i]): return match(s, i+1, p, j+1); return False.

examples.trace: p[0]='a' matches s[0]='a' → call match(s,1,p,1). p[1]='*' → branch skip: match(s,1,p,2) → j=2 end, i=1 not end → false; branch use: match(s,2,p,1) → p[1]='*' → skip: match(s,2,p,2) → j=2 end, i=2 end → true. Return true.
p[0]='?' and i=0 < len(s) → call match(s,1,p,1). j=1 end, i=1 end → true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision Making at '*' Characters
ladders.desc: Handling the '*' wildcard by exploring both skip and match options.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of the brute force approach is how to handle '*' characters, which introduce non-determinism. Intuition: '*' can match zero or more characters, so at each '*', we must try both possibilities—either skip it (match zero characters) or use it to match the current character and keep it active for potentially more matches. This creates a binary choice, leading to exponential exploration. The algorithm for this logic: when at pattern index j with '*', make two recursive calls: one with j+1 (skip the '*'), and one with i+1 (if i not at end) and j unchanged (use '*' to match current char and keep it). This ensures all possible lengths of matching are considered. Time complexity for this decision point is O(2^k) where k is number of '*' in relevant suffix, but per call it's O(1). Space is O(1) plus recursion. Connection to L3: This logic relies on comparing characters (for non-'*' cases) from L3. Common mistakes: trying to match '*' to exactly one character only (missing skip option), or not keeping j unchanged when using '*' (which would incorrectly consume it). Real code pattern: if p[j] == '*': return recursive_call(s, i, p, j+1) or (i < len(s) and recursive_call(s, i+1, p, j)).

examples.trace: p[0]='*' → branch skip: call with j=1 → p[1]='b', s[0]='a' no match → false; branch use: call with i=1, j=0 → p[0]='*' again → branch skip: j=1 → p[1]='b', s[1]='b' match → i=2,j=2 ends → true. Return true.
p[0]='*' → branch skip: j=1 end, i=0 not end → false; branch use: i=1, j=0 → p[0]='*' → branch skip: j=1 end, i=1 end → true. Return true.

ladders.title: Building Block — Character Comparison with '?' Wildcard
ladders.desc: Matching single characters exactly or with '?' wildcard.
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the simple case of matching a single character: either an exact match between pattern character and string character, or a match with '?' which matches any single character. Intuition: before dealing with '*', we need to correctly handle direct character comparisons—this is the deterministic part of the algorithm. The algorithm: given indices i and j, if pattern[j] is '?', it matches any character as long as i is within string bounds, so we can advance both pointers. If pattern[j] is a regular character, it must exactly equal string[i] (with i in bounds) to advance. Otherwise, match fails. This is a fundamental step that reduces the problem size by one character each time. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This uses basic string indexing from L4. Common mistakes: not checking i < len(s) before accessing s[i], which causes index errors, or treating '?' as matching zero characters (it must match exactly one). Real code pattern: if i < len(s) and (p[j] == '?' or p[j] == s[i]): return recursive_call(s, i+1, p, j+1).

examples.trace: p[0]='c' matches s[0]='c' → i=1,j=1. p[1]='?' matches s[1]='a' → i=2,j=2. p[2]='t' matches s[2]='t' → i=3,j=3 ends → true.
p[0]='d' matches s[0]='d' → i=1,j=1. p[1]='?' matches s[1]='o' → i=2,j=2. p[2]='g' matches s[2]='g' → i=3,j=3 ends → true.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Indexing and Bounds Checking
ladders.desc: Accessing characters in strings and checking index limits.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves safely accessing characters from the string and pattern using indices i and j, while ensuring we don't go out of bounds. Intuition: recursion progresses by moving pointers i and j through the string and pattern; we must check if i is within [0, len(s)-1] before accessing s[i] to avoid errors, and similarly for j with p[j]. The algorithm: before any character access, verify i < len(s) for string and j < len(p) for pattern. In base cases, we check if j == len(p) to see if pattern is exhausted, and if i == len(s) for string exhaustion. This is crucial for termination and correct matching. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space is O(1). Connection to L5: This builds on understanding strings as indexed sequences from L5. Common mistakes: accessing s[i] without bounds check, leading to IndexError, or using <= instead of < for indices. Real code pattern: if j == len(p): return i == len(s); if i < len(s) and j < len(p): # proceed with comparisons.

examples.trace: j=0 == len(p)=0 → check i=2 == len(s)=2? Yes → but wait, base case: if j==len(p), return i==len(s). Here i=2, len(s)=2, so true? Correction: s='hi', len=2, i=2 is out of bounds (indices 0,1), so i==len(s) means end of string. Actually, for s='hi', i=2 means past end, so i==len(s) is true, but in context, if pattern is empty and string at end, it's a match. But example says output false—let's clarify: input is s='hi', i=2, p='', j=0. j=0 == len(p)=0 → return i==len(s). i=2, len(s)=2 → true. So output should be true. To make false: s='hi', i=1, p='', j=0 → j=0 end, i=1 not end → false. Let's use that: s='hi', i=1, p='', j=0 → j=0 end, i=1 not end → false.
i=0 < len(s)=1, j=0 < len(p)=1 → p[0]='b', s[0]='a' no match → false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Recursion Base Cases
ladders.desc: Basic string concepts and simple recursive termination conditions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers two prerequisites: strings as sequences of characters accessible by index (0-based), and the idea of recursion base cases—conditions that stop the recursion. Intuition: in wildcard matching, we traverse strings using indices, so understanding that s[0] is first character, s[len(s)-1] is last, and s[len(s)] is out of bounds is essential. For recursion, base cases define when the problem is solved trivially: if pattern is empty, match only if string is also empty; if string is empty but pattern isn't, match only if pattern is all '*' (handled in higher levels). The algorithm: define strings s and p with lengths m and n. Indices i and j start at 0. Base case: if j == n, return i == m. This is the simplest termination check. Time complexity is O(1) for this check. Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This provides the groundwork for bounds checking. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in indices, or misunderstanding empty string representation. Real code pattern: def base_case(i, j): if j == len(p): return i == len(s).

examples.trace: i=0, j=0. len(s)=0, len(p)=0. j=0 == len(p)=0 → check i=0 == len(s)=0 → true.
i=0, j=0. len(p)=0 → j=0 == 0 → check i=0 == len(s)=5? No → false.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming 2D Table O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Build a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] indicates if the first i characters of the pattern match the first j characters of the string. Handle '*' by allowing it to match zero or more characters via state transitions. This is a standard DP solution that guarantees correctness for all cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Wildcard Matching with 2D DP Table
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of wildcard pattern matching using a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] indicates if first i chars of pattern match first j chars of string.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this DP solution is to break down the matching problem into smaller subproblems. We create a 2D boolean table dp where dp[i][j] represents whether the first i characters of the pattern match the first j characters of the string. The key insight is that we can build the solution incrementally by considering how each character in the pattern interacts with each character in the string. For regular characters, we require exact match. For '?', it matches any single character. For '*', it can match zero or more characters, which is handled by allowing the star to either match nothing (carrying forward the match from previous pattern position) or match one more character (carrying forward from previous string position). The algorithm starts with dp[0][0] = True (empty pattern matches empty string). For each pattern position i and string position j: 1) If pattern[i-1] is '*', then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR dp[i][j-1] (match zero characters OR match one more character). 2) If pattern[i-1] is '?' or equals string[j-1], then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]. Otherwise dp[i][j] = False. The final answer is dp[m][n] where m is pattern length and n is string length. Time complexity is O(m*n) for filling the table. Space complexity is O(m*n) for the DP table. This connects to L1 as the complete implementation of the DP state transitions. Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize dp[0][0] = True, incorrectly handling the '*' transition (must consider both zero and one+ matches), and off-by-one errors in indices. The real code pattern involves nested loops over pattern and string lengths with conditional logic for each character type.

examples.trace: Pattern length m=3, string length n=3. Initialize dp[4][4] table. dp[0][0]=True. Fill row 0: dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=False, dp[0][3]=False. Fill column 0: dp[1][0]=False (pattern 'a' doesn't match empty string), dp[2][0]=dp[1][0]=False ('*' matches zero chars), dp[3][0]=False. Now fill rest: i=1,j=1: pattern[0]='a', string[0]='a' match, dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=1,j=2: 'a' vs 'c' no match, dp[1][2]=False. i=1,j=3: 'a' vs 'b' no match, dp[1][3]=False. i=2,j=1: pattern[1]='*', dp[2][1]=dp[1][1] OR dp[2][0]=True OR False=True. i=2,j=2: '*', dp[2][2]=dp[1][2] OR dp[2][1]=False OR True=True. i=2,j=3: '*', dp[2][3]=dp[1][3] OR dp[2][2]=False OR True=True. i=3,j=1: pattern[2]='b', string[0]='a' no match, dp[3][1]=False. i=3,j=2: 'b' vs 'c' no match, dp[3][2]=False. i=3,j=3: 'b' vs 'b' match, dp[3][3]=dp[2][2]=True. Final dp[3][3]=True.

m=2, n=2. Initialize dp[3][3]. dp[0][0]=True. Row 0: dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=False. Column 0: dp[1][0]=False, dp[2][0]=False. i=1,j=1: pattern[0]='?', matches any char 'b', dp[1][1]=dp[0][0]=True. i=1,j=2: '?' matches 'a', dp[1][2]=dp[0][1]=False. i=2,j=1: pattern[1]='a', string[0]='b' no match, dp[2][1]=False. i=2,j=2: 'a' vs 'a' match, dp[2][2]=dp[1][1]=True. Final dp[2][2]=True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Transition for '*' Character
ladders.desc: Implement the state transition logic for handling '*' wildcard in the DP table.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for handling '*' is that it represents zero or more characters, which requires considering two possibilities in the DP transition: either the star matches zero characters (so we ignore it and carry forward the match from the previous pattern position) or it matches one more character (so we carry forward from the previous string position while keeping the star active). Mathematically, if pattern[i-1] = '*', then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR dp[i][j-1]. dp[i-1][j] represents matching zero characters (the star consumes nothing), while dp[i][j-1] represents matching one more character (the star consumes string[j-1] and remains active). This recursive relationship allows stars to match any sequence. The algorithm for this sub-routine is: given dp table filled up to row i-1 and column j-1, and current positions i (pattern index) and j (string index), if pattern[i-1] == '*', set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] OR dp[i][j-1]. Time complexity for this operation is O(1) per cell. Space complexity is O(1) additional beyond the table. This connects to L0 as the most critical part of the full solution—incorrect star handling breaks the entire algorithm. Common mistakes include: using AND instead of OR, forgetting to check both possibilities, or incorrectly implementing the indices. The real code pattern is a simple conditional check with boolean OR.

examples.input: pattern: '*', string: 'abc', i=1, j=1 (matching first char 'a')
examples.trace: We have dp table with dp[0][0]=True, dp[0][1]=False, dp[0][2]=False, dp[0][3]=False, and dp[1][0]=False. For i=1 (pattern index 0 is '*'), j=1 (string index 0 is 'a'): dp[i-1][j] = dp[0][1] = False (matching zero chars from empty pattern). dp[i][j-1] = dp[1][0] = False (matching one more char from empty string). dp[1][1] = False OR False = False. Wait, correction: Actually dp[1][0] should be True because '*' matches empty string. Let's recalc: Initialize properly: dp[0][0]=True. For i=1,j=0: pattern[0]='*', dp[1][0]=dp[0][0] OR dp[1][-1] (invalid), so just dp[0][0]=True. Now for i=1,j=1: dp[0][1]=False, dp[1][0]=True, so dp[1][1]=False OR True=True. So '*' matches 'a'.

examples.input: pattern: 'a*', string: 'a', i=2, j=1 (after matching 'a', star at end)
examples.trace: Assume dp[1][1]=True (pattern 'a' matches string 'a'). For i=2 (pattern index 1 is '*'), j=1 (string index 0 is 'a', already consumed): dp[i-1][j] = dp[1][1] = True (star matches zero chars, so pattern 'a' matches 'a'). dp[i][j-1] = dp[2][0]. Need dp[2][0]: pattern 'a*' vs empty string: dp[1][0]=False ('a' doesn't match empty), dp[2][0]=dp[1][0] OR dp[2][-1]=False. So dp[2][1] = True OR False = True. So 'a*' matches 'a'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Table Initialization and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Set up the DP table with correct initial values for empty pattern and empty string cases.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for initialization is that we need a starting point for the DP recurrence. The base case is that an empty pattern matches only an empty string. Therefore, dp[0][0] = True. For other positions: dp[i][0] (pattern of length i vs empty string) is True only if all characters in pattern[0..i-1] are '*' (since stars can match zero characters). dp[0][j] (empty pattern vs string of length j) is always False because empty pattern cannot match any non-empty string. This initialization ensures the DP transitions have valid starting values. The algorithm: create dp table of size (m+1) x (n+1). Set dp[0][0] = True. For i from 1 to m: if pattern[i-1] == '*', then dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0]; else dp[i][0] = False. For j from 1 to n: dp[0][j] = False. Time complexity O(m+n) for initialization. Space complexity O(m*n) for the table. This connects to L1 as it provides the initial state for transitions. Common mistakes include: forgetting to set dp[0][0]=True, incorrectly computing dp[i][0] for non-star patterns, or using wrong dimensions. The real code pattern involves loops for first row and column.

examples.trace: m=2, n=2. Create dp[3][3]. Set dp[0][0]=True. Initialize first column: i=1, pattern[0]='a' not '*', so dp[1][0]=False. i=2, pattern[1]='*', so dp[2][0]=dp[1][0]=False. Initialize first row: j=1, dp[0][1]=False. j=2, dp[0][2]=False. Table initially: dp[0][0]=T, others F.
m=2, n=0. Create dp[3][1]. dp[0][0]=True. First column: i=1, pattern[0]='*', so dp[1][0]=dp[0][0]=True. i=2, pattern[1]='a' not '*', so dp[2][0]=False. First row not needed as n=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matching Single Characters with '?' Wildcard
ladders.desc: Handle exact character matches and the '?' wildcard that matches any single character.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for non-star characters, matching is straightforward: either the characters are identical, or the pattern has '?' which matches any character. In DP terms, if pattern[i-1] is not '*', then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] IF pattern[i-1] == '?' OR pattern[i-1] == string[j-1]. This means the current match depends on whether all previous characters matched. The algorithm: for given i and j, if pattern[i-1] != '*', check if pattern[i-1] == '?' or pattern[i-1] == string[j-1]. If true, set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; else set dp[i][j] = False. Time complexity O(1) per check. Space complexity O(1) additional. This connects to L2 as part of the DP transition logic alongside star handling. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check both '?' and equality, using wrong indices for dp[i-1][j-1], or not handling case sensitivity (usually case-sensitive unless specified). The real code pattern is an if-else with a condition.

examples.trace: Assume dp[0][0]=True. pattern[0]='a', string[0]='a', they match exactly. So dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] = True.
dp[0][0]=True. pattern[0]='?', matches any char 'b'. So dp[1][1] = dp[0][0] = True.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Updating 2D DP Table Cells
ladders.desc: Work with a 2D boolean table, setting and getting values at specific indices.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a 2D DP table is a grid where each cell dp[i][j] stores a boolean value representing a subproblem result. We need to correctly index into this table using row and column indices. The table has dimensions (m+1) x (n+1) where m is pattern length and n is string length, to include the empty case. Rows correspond to pattern prefixes (0 to m), columns to string prefixes (0 to n). The basic operations are: 1) Initialize table with all False. 2) Set dp[0][0] = True. 3) For any i,j, get value dp[i][j] or set dp[i][j] to True/False. The algorithm involves creating a list of lists in Python: dp = [[False]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]. Then dp[0][0] = True. To access: value = dp[i][j]. To update: dp[i][j] = True or False. Time complexity O(1) per access/update. Space complexity O(m*n) for the table. This connects to L3 as the data structure used for storing match results. Common mistakes include: using wrong dimensions (off-by-one), confusing row/column order, or shallow copying lists incorrectly. The real code pattern is list comprehension for table creation.

examples.output: dp table with True at (0,0) and (1,1), False elsewhere
examples.trace: Create dp = [[False, False, False], [False, False, False], [False, False, False]]. Set dp[0][0] = True. Set dp[1][1] = True. Result: row0: [True, False, False]; row1: [False, True, False]; row2: [False, False, False].

examples.input: m=1, n=1, initialize and set dp[1][1] based on condition
examples.trace: Create dp = [[False, False], [False, False]]. Set dp[0][0]=True. Check some condition: if True, set dp[1][1]=True. Final: [[True, False], [False, True]].

testCases.input: m=1, n=2, check dp[0][2] is False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Wildcard Characters '*' and '?'
ladders.desc: Learn what '*' and '?' mean in pattern matching: '*' matches zero or more characters, '?' matches any single character.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that wildcards are special characters that make pattern matching flexible. '?' acts as a placeholder for exactly one character—it can match any single character from the string. '*' is more powerful: it can match any sequence of characters, including an empty sequence. For example, pattern 'a?c' matches 'abc', 'axc', etc., but not 'ac' (missing one char) or 'abbc' (too many chars). Pattern 'a*c' matches 'ac', 'abc', 'abbc', etc., because '*' can match zero or more characters between 'a' and 'c'. This is the foundation for the DP approach: we need to handle these wildcards systematically. The algorithm for this level is just understanding: given a pattern and string, check mentally if they match based on these rules. Time and space complexity are not applicable here. This connects to L4 as the problem definition that the DP table solves. Common mistakes include: thinking '*' matches exactly one char, or '?' matches multiple chars. The real code pattern is not needed yet—just conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Pattern: h e ? l o. String: h e l l o. '?' matches 'l'. All other chars match exactly. So True.
Pattern: h e * l o. String: h e x x y l o. '*' matches 'xxy'. So True.

approaches.name: Greedy Two Pointer O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers for the pattern and string, backtracking only to the last '*' position when a mismatch occurs, allowing linear time by greedily matching characters. It's optimal for most practical inputs but requires careful handling of edge cases with multiple '*'.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Greedy Two Pointer Wildcard Matching
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of wildcard pattern matching using two pointers with backtracking to last '*' position.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the greedy two-pointer approach for wildcard pattern matching. The core intuition is that '*' can match any sequence (including empty), so we can greedily match characters when possible, and only backtrack to the last '*' position when we encounter a mismatch. We maintain two pointers: i for the string and j for the pattern. When characters match or pattern has '?', we advance both. When pattern has '*', we record the positions (star_i, star_j) and advance only the pattern pointer (treating '*' as matching empty sequence initially). On mismatch, if we have a recorded '*', we backtrack: increment star_i (meaning '*' consumes one more character from string), reset i to star_i, and j to star_j+1. If no '*' to backtrack to, matching fails. After processing all characters, we skip any trailing '*' in pattern. The algorithm runs in O(m+n) time and O(1) space because each pointer moves forward at most m+n times with constant backtracking. This approach is optimal for most practical cases but requires careful handling of multiple '*' and edge cases like empty strings. Common mistakes include not properly resetting pointers during backtracking, forgetting to skip trailing '*', and incorrect handling of consecutive '*'. The key insight is that we only need to remember the last '*' position since earlier '*' can be subsumed by later ones in greedy matching.

examples.trace:
Initialize: i=0, j=0, star_i=-1, star_j=-1
Step1: p[0]='*', record star_i=0, star_j=0, j=1
Step2: s[0]='a', p[1]='a', match, i=1, j=2
Step3: s[1]='d', p[2]='*', record star_i=1, star_j=2, j=3
Step4: s[1]='d', p[3]='b', mismatch, backtrack: star_i=2, i=2, j=3
Step5: s[2]='c', p[3]='b', mismatch, backtrack: star_i=3, i=3, j=3
Step6: s[3]='e', p[3]='b', mismatch, backtrack: star_i=4, i=4, j=3
Step7: s[4]='b', p[3]='b', match, i=5, j=4
End string: i=5, j=4, skip trailing: none
Result: true

Initialize: i=0, j=0, star_i=-1, star_j=-1
Step1: s[0]='a', p[0]='a', match, i=1, j=1
Step2: p[1]='*', record star_i=1, star_j=1, j=2
Step3: s[1]='c', p[2]='c', match, i=2, j=3
Step4: s[2]='d', p[3]='?', match any, i=3, j=4
Step5: s[3]='c', p[4]='b', mismatch, backtrack: star_i=2, i=2, j=2
Step6: s[2]='d', p[2]='c', mismatch, backtrack: star_i=3, i=3, j=2
Step7: s[3]='c', p[2]='c', match, i=4, j=3
Step8: s[4]='b', p[3]='?', match any, i=5, j=4
Step9: s[4]='b', p[4]='b', match, i=5, j=5
End string: i=5, j=5, skip trailing: none
But wait: after step8, i=5, j=4, then step9 tries s[4] which is out of bounds! Actually trace correction: At step8, i=4, j=3: s[4]='b', p[3]='?', match, i=5, j=4. Then at step9, i=5 (end), j=4, p[4]='b', no character to match, so false. The algorithm would detect this when checking i < len(s) in main loop.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking to Last Star Position
ladders.desc: Implement the backtracking mechanism when mismatch occurs with recorded '*' position.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core backtracking logic of the greedy approach. When we encounter a mismatch between string character s[i] and pattern character p[j], and we have previously recorded a '*' position (star_j != -1), we need to backtrack. The intuition is that the recorded '*' can consume one more character from the string to try to make the match work. We increment star_i (the string position where '*' started matching), reset i to this new star_i, and reset j to star_j+1 (just after the '*'). This effectively makes the '*' match one more character than before. If no '*' is recorded, matching fails. The time complexity is O(1) per backtrack operation, and in total O(m+n) across the algorithm. Space is O(1) for storing star positions. This feeds into the full problem by providing the recovery mechanism when greedy matching fails. Common mistakes include not incrementing star_i before resetting i (leading to infinite loops), or resetting j to star_j instead of star_j+1. The key insight is that we only backtrack to the last '*' because earlier '*' positions become irrelevant once we've moved past them in greedy matching.

examples.input: Current: i=2, j=3, star_i=1, star_j=1, s='abc', p='a*c'
examples.trace:
Initial state: i=2 (s[2]='c'), j=3 (p[3] out of bounds or mismatch), star_i=1, star_j=1
Step1: Increment star_i: star_i=2
Step2: Reset i to star_i: i=2
Step3: Reset j to star_j+1: j=2
Result: i=2, j=2, star_i=2

examples.input: Current: i=3, j=4, star_i=-1, star_j=-1, s='ab', p='a*b'
examples.trace:
Initial state: i=3 (out of bounds), j=4 (out of bounds), star_i=-1, star_j=-1
Check: star_j == -1, so no '*' recorded
Result: return false (cannot backtrack)

testCases.input: i=1, j=2, star_i=0, star_j=0, s='ab', p='a*b'
i=2, j=3, star_i=1, star_j=1, s='abc', p='a*c'
i=0, j=1, star_i=-1, star_j=-1, s='a', p='b'
i=3, j=4, star_i=2, star_j=2, s='abcd', p='a*d'
i=5, j=6, star_i=4, star_j=4, s='abcdef', p='a*f'
i=2, j=3, star_i=0, star_j=0, s='ab', p='*b'
i=4, j=5, star_i=3, star_j=3, s='abcde', p='a*e'
i=1, j=2, star_i=0, star_j=0, s='aa', p='*a'
i=3, j=4, star_i=2, star_j=2, s='aaa', p='*a'

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Matching with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement the main loop that advances pointers based on character matches, '?', and '*'.
ladders.explanation: This implements the central greedy matching algorithm using two pointers i (string index) and j (pattern index). The logic: while i < len(s), if j < len(p) and (s[i] == p[j] or p[j] == '?'), it's a match—advance both pointers. Else if p[j] == '*', record star positions (star_i=i, star_j=j) and advance only j (treating '*' as matching empty initially). Else (mismatch), if we have a recorded '*', backtrack using the sub-routine from L1. If no '*', return false. After the loop, skip any trailing '*' in pattern by advancing j while p[j] == '*'. Finally, return true if j == len(p) (pattern fully consumed). The intuition is greedy: match as much as possible directly, use '*' only when necessary, and backtrack minimally. Time O(m+n), space O(1). This feeds into L0 as the main algorithm body. Common mistakes: not checking j < len(p) before accessing p[j], incorrect order of conditions (check '*' before mismatch), or not handling trailing '*'. The key insight is that by greedily matching and only backtracking to last '*', we achieve linear time.

examples.trace:
i=0, j=0: s[0]='a', p[0]='a', match, i=1, j=1
i=1, j=1: p[1]='*', record star_i=1, star_j=1, j=2
Loop ends (i=1 < len(s)=2? Wait i=1, len(s)=2, so continue)
i=1, j=2: j >= len(p)=2, so mismatch, backtrack: star_j=1 != -1, star_i=2, i=2, j=2
Loop ends (i=2 >= len(s)=2)
Skip trailing '*': j=2, no trailing
Check j=2 == len(p)=2, true

i=0, j=0: s[0]='a', p[0]='a', match, i=1, j=1
i=1, j=1: s[1] out of bounds? i=1, len(s)=1, so loop ends
Skip trailing: j=1, p[1]='b' not '*'
Check j=1 != len(p)=2, false

ladders.title: Building Block — Handling Wildcard Characters '?' and '*'
ladders.desc: Implement logic to process wildcard characters in pattern matching.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on how to handle the two wildcard characters: '?' matches any single character, and '*' matches any sequence of characters (including empty). For '?', when encountered in pattern at position j, it matches whatever character is at string position i, so we simply advance both pointers. For '*', it's more complex: we record its position (star_i, star_j) to allow backtracking later, and initially advance only the pattern pointer (j), treating '*' as matching empty sequence. This greedy approach defers matching characters to '*' until necessary. The intuition is that '?' is straightforward—one-to-one matching—while '*' requires state management for potential backtracking. Time O(1) per character, space O(1) for star positions. This feeds into L2 as part of the main matching logic. Common mistakes: not advancing pointers correctly for '?', or for '*', advancing both pointers initially (which would over-consume). The key insight is separating the handling: '?' is deterministic, '*' is non-deterministic requiring backtracking.

examples.trace:
i=0, j=0: p[0]='?', matches any char s[0]='a'
Action: i=1, j=1

i=0, j=0: p[0]='*'
Action: record star_i=0, star_j=0, j=1 (i stays 0)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison with Wildcards
ladders.desc: Compare a string character with a pattern character, handling exact match and '?'.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation compares a single character from the string (s[i]) with a single character from the pattern (p[j]). It returns true if: (1) p[j] is a regular character and equals s[i], or (2) p[j] is '?' (which matches any single character). It returns false otherwise. Note that '*' is not handled here—it's treated separately at higher levels. The intuition is simple: we need a fundamental matching rule for non-wildcard and '?' cases. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 as part of wildcard handling. Common mistakes: not checking array bounds before access, or confusing '?' with '*'. The key insight is that character comparison is the atomic operation of pattern matching.

examples.trace: p[0]='a' is regular char, s[0]='a', equal, so true
p[0]='?' matches any char, s[0]='b', so true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Traversal with Pointers
ladders.desc: Iterate through strings using index pointers, comparing characters sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves traversing two strings simultaneously using integer pointers (indices) i and j. Start with i=0, j=0. While i < len(string1) and j < len(string2), compare characters at current positions. If they match, increment both pointers to move to next characters. If not, handle according to specific rules (not defined here). The intuition is linear scanning: we process strings from left to right, one character at a time. Time O(n) for strings of length n, space O(1) for pointers. This feeds into L4 as the framework for character comparison. Common mistakes: forgetting to check array bounds, off-by-one errors, or not incrementing pointers correctly. The key insight is that many string algorithms use this simple pointer traversal as a basis.

examples.trace:
i=0,j=0: 'h'='h', i=1,j=1
i=1,j=1: 'e'='e', i=2,j=2
i=2,j=2: 'l'='l', i=3,j=3
i=3,j=3: 'l'='l', i=4,j=4
i=4,j=4: 'o'='o', i=5,j=5
End: both pointers at end, full match
